speaker: interviewer
text: OK, January 24, 1996. Interview is with Henry Moser, last name at birth Moser, interviewed by Maureen Halpert. It's Westminster, California, USA. We're speaking English, tape number one.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Maureen. That's M-A-U-R-E-E-N, Halpert, H-A-L-P-- as in Paul-- E-R-T. I'm interviewing Henry Moser. We are in Westminster, California, USA. The language is English, and it's January 24th, 1996. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Can you give me your full name, and spell it for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Henry E. Moser. H-E-N-R-Y E for Ernst, E-R-N-S-T, M-O-S-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Heinz Ernst Moser.

speaker: interviewer
text: And spell that, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: H-E-I-N-Z E-R-N-S-T M-O-S-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you used any other nicknames or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- can you-- what is the city and the country where you were born, and spell it for me, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Frankfurt-am-- on Main, Germany. F-- F-R-A-N-K-F-U-R-T O-M, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Germany, G-E-R-M-A-N-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what date were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tell me again where you were born, where you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Frankfurt, and when we were-- I was what? Four and a half years old, we moved to Berlin. And I grew up in Berlin until I was 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alfred Moser and Lily Moser.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do for work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was an advertising salesman. He worked for a-- I guess you would call it a trade paper, which was an elec-- electrical-- all-electrical company, including the-- it would go to the appliance dealer, to the contractors, to the wholesalers. It covered the industry, which included which from-- from radio-- not yet television-- to all the major appliances, minor appliances, to cables, for instance. Anything to do with-- with electric contracting. He had some customers which are now world known, like Siemens AG, OSRAM, Telefunken, Philips, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and Beckman Electric, and a few other names-- maybe lesser known names. But he-- he made very good contract before the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your mother? Did she work, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my mother was accepted-- she was helping him a little bit with letter writing and so on, but basically she was a housewife. Before she got ma-- before she was married, she worked at a bank, for-- as a clerk in the office, and later on in a photo studio.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lilly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the house like where you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were a close family in that way. We were a close family when I grew up in Frankfurt, with the grandparents. As a matter of fact, we lived in my grandparents' apartment house. We had an apartment in my grandparents' house. I was even born, I understand, in the house. A doctor came to the house. And-- and we saw my other grandparents at least once a week or so. I remember we were there for dinner, or lunch, really, which was a major meal. And my father's other brother and sister-in-law were there, usually. And I remember I had-- I remember my grandfather, who passed away when I was only a little over three years old. But I remember him well, because I remember having-- sitting on his lap, and he gave me some food I liked, whatever it was. And I remember talking with him, from one room to another. They had an extension phone.

speaker: interviewer
text: My grandfather did basically the same thing my father did. I'm not sure exactly what, and if was an electrical or was it in steel? I-- I really don't remember at that time, because it was an earlier time, and electric appliances were pretty new at that time. But he did-- he was an advertising salesman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had a big-- they had a big apartment, I remember, and like I said, and I-- I remember talking with him from one to the other, with an extension phone-- the big old phones with the wooden receivers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your grandparents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- my grandfather's name was Sigfried and Sophie Moser, and the other grandparents, their name was Johanna and Edward Schott, S-C-H-O-T-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Jewish life? Can you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My earliest Jewish life, I remember, was, of course, the synagogue. And we were not religious, but they were more or less going to the synagogue-- as far as I remember, frequently, but not-- certainly not every week. They were not-- they went, to the holy days, at least, they went. And I remember the synagogue, the people with the high silk hats, still. I remember that, when I was li-- when I-- when I was little. And I learned the concept of God very early, because I do remember when I-- when we went to lunch, to my grandmother's house one day, and my grandfather wasn't there. And I remember asking where he was. She said-- she just told me, God took him with him. And I accepted that. And-- and it was nothing horrible in my eyes at the time. It was accepted. I meet people, even today, that want to keep death away from the children, not even tell them that the dog died, which I think is ridiculous. I don't think it ever hurt me in my life. I never was-- talk about religious, I never was-- was-- was that religious, but I believed in God, and I believed God was something good. That's what I was taught.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe your family upbringing? Was it Conservative, Reform, or Orthodox? How would you describe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: More in the liberal part of conservative. In other words, they did not keep a kosher household anymore. Also, I understand, my-- my great-grandparents, on one side, at least, did. But they lived-- some of them lived in smaller towns before, and they were far more traditional. In my grandparents' time, they lived in a big city, and they were not that traditional. Nevertheless, they were-- they were not bad Jews. I considered them, they were good Jews, because I don't think good and bad, in my opinion, has anything to do with being religious. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of things did you enjoy doing as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of things did you enjoy doing as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My mother had a passport to the zoo, and when-- and when she didn't have to work-- and we had a maid-- and-- and-- and she went-- the way I understand, and I remember, of course, some of it-- she went to the zoo with me, sometimes three, four times a week. I played with neighbors' kids. This is now in Frankfurt, before I was five years old. And I always had all the toys I ever wished for. Maybe not as many as others, but I-- I wasn't-- there was no-- no-- no need. I-- I remember that I was a happy child. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] One incident, is-- which has been probably influenced my life negatively, I remember I-- my parents went, usually Saturday afternoons, to cafe house, which was-- in Europe, cafe house was entertainment. They had musicians there. It was relaxing, stay a couple of hours. And you could take kids along. A lot of these cafe houses had-- had-- had two rooms, one with music, one without. And-- and I-- I liked music, evidently, even as a kid. And I was going in front of this little three or four piece combo, and was conducting him. And one drummer gave me a little conducting stick. And one day, a man played a cello, and I'll never forget that. He used his bow, and just tipped me on the head. For him, it was funny, but he didn't smile. And I screamed and ran away, and never did this again. And I think I became shyer from that time on, but I don't know. But it stands out in my mind as some-- as something bad. I don't think the man meant any bad, to be quite sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: The children that you played with, were they Jewish, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin, there were some. Most of them were not. We lived in Berlin in-- in a middle class neighborhood. Like I said, there were about four or five Jewish kids living in-- in-- in the complex where we lived, and we had a huge backyard there. And we-- about the size of two football fields. And it was basically built-- was newly built-- for yo-- for a young family with children. And we-- I had a ball there. And there were some Jewish kids there, yes, and most of them were not. And I cannot remember having had any real problem with any of those kids, until the Nazi time. And not even then, with those who know me from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when your family moved to Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So that was in 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the neighborhood like, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin? It was a nice neighborhood. It was a middle-- middle class neighborhood. I wouldn't say upper middle class, but it was a neighborhood where most people were-- were fairly well to do. But there were poor people there. This wasn't like in America. It wasn't that segregated. Certainly, there lived some-- some poor people was in that neighborhood. I had no problem in the school. There were, I know, in our class of about 40, there was two other Jewish boys. That's all. Neither one were friends of mine, and I-- basically I-- there wasn't a need to make friends in school. My parents told me I should, really, because we may not always live here. Because I had my friends right around, living where-- in-- where I lived. And they didn't necessarily go to school class with me, even though they may have attended the same school. So no, I had no problems at all at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody knew you were Jewish? Did all-- did the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your schoolmates know that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew. Oh yes, they definitely knew, because-- because [INAUDIBLE] they used to read, in the class, they ask everybody's religion when you started a new class, or a new semester. The teacher had-- had to fill out a form, and religion was one of the questions they asked. And everybody was-- was usually Christian-- Catholic, or most-- most of them in Berlin were Protestant, or Lutheran. Evang-- Evangelical. They called it evangelist. And-- and they-- I remember one kid in my class, said they called themselves dissidents. Dissidents were agnostics. They came from, probably, Christian families, but they were agnostics. They didn't believe in any religion, but they lived-- believed in God. So I guess you call it agnostics here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember only one boy, and people didn't make a big thing out of it. The kids did not make a big thing out of it, till-- until they were instigated by the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of languages did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak any other languages?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in school, what type of subjects did you study?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elementary school was reading, writing, arithmetic, a-- a little bit of geography. This-- this is-- you learned to read the map about your neighborhood, and-- and start from then-- from-- from that. And then you had art classes, and-- and gym, and music classes. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Though otherwise-- and when I went to-- at ten years, you go to a higher school, which is voluntary. The school system was-- you could stay eight years in the-- in the elementary school. But in-- in the neighborhood I lived, about two-thirds of the kids left, and went to a higher school. And I went to a school, a 12-year school, [INAUDIBLE] similar to a high school over here, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] geared to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to almost any profession you wanted to, but with the exception of medical doctors went to another school where Greek and Latin was required, or one was required, and this school, it was voluntary. It was a little easier, maybe, but basically they gave you all the science and so on-- and geography, geology. Actually, we studied geology, and they gave me foreign languages. I started to learn French when I was 10 years old. We had French classes. That was not voluntary. You had to have one language, and-- and-- and they didn't give you even a choice in this school. When they decided to start Fre-- in French in this class, you took French. In other schools, they may have decided to start English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know whether the school system over there was-- was run by the-- basically by the, I believe, the state. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I'm not sure about this, and I understand it must have changed totally by now-- an entirely different system now, closer to ours, here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called [GERMAN]. And the name of the street, [? Eliezenstrasse ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was in Berlin, in a suburb of Berlin, Steg-- called Steglitz. The school is today called just Oberschule, which means high school, upper school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six months, because after six months, they gave me-- my-- my father had lost his job, because he worked for a paper-- that paper he worked for had a lot of featured articles, and it fell under-- under, like all newspapers. Like all public, not private, newspapers. This was considered a public newspaper, under-- directly under the propaganda ministry of Dr. Goebbels. And Jews were not-- were-- were the first ones to be laid off. Jews could not work on those papers. Private papers, yes. This was not considered private. Now, I cannot explain the-- I'm not sure why not. It was called-- the German word was Reichsverband Deutsche Elektrotechnische. And I have a document there, where they gave my father a recommendation. They gave him a recommendation, even. But-- so we didn't have any money. However, my father had collected enough ads, actually, for two years ahead of time, because he was very friendly with the public relations officer of some of the big companies. They gave him two year ahead ads. But he got a commission. So my father said to the company-- asked the company-- give me 10% of that money so I can leave the country, because he was ready, willing, and able to leave the country in 1933. And they said, no way. We pay you monthly as we take it in, which they did. But so we-- we had no money saved. Business was pretty bad, as was the-- as-- as-- as the time preceding was bad, like-- like the Depression over here. Over there, it was certainly as bad, too. Only the thing was that just-- just prices went up, and up, and up, and people didn't have any money, so it amounted to the same thing. And so I got a scholarship still, in the spring of 1933, when I went to started going [INAUDIBLE] the high school. And they cancelled that scholarship, but they told me, since my father was a veteran of World War I, they would-- I could stay. But it cost so-- so much a month, and it was a considerable amount. I believe it was 35 Marks, something like this-- the full amount, not even reduced. So my father took me out of the school. [INAUDIBLE] my father was teaching at a private Jewish school, and they got me in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father doing for the newspaper?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- a-- advertising salesman. In other words, he was selling the ads to the big companies. They had big-- lots of big ads in there. And, as you-- you know, historically or whatever, that was a time, the early '30s was a time, when electrical appliances, electric equipment was really going into high gear. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Motors, everything, you know-- it-- it-- it had a tremendous future.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did he do when he left?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked for a short time for a private paper, which was a-- a-- a kind of a steel-- similar, in the steel industry, but it was a small, Jewish owned paper. But after a year or so, that man folded up, too-- the owner of it. Naturally, the Nazis did everything to pressure papers like this out-- out of business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was-- if he didn't close, he got very small, so there was-- there was nothing there anymore. Then my father worked for-- for three years for the-- as a volunteer, because they don't give you a job, as a Jew. If you-- if you-- you know, if you-- a job was-- another job to get was almost impossible under the circumstances. So he worked for-- for three years at the voluntary for the Jewish community council. The Youth and Welfare Office, they called that. It was a branch office in our neighborhood. And he got lunch there. That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were lucky. Meanwhile, my grandmother had passed away, and she had a large apartment house in Frankfurt-- the house I was born in. And there was enough income, and mother's two brothers decided in-- that they pay us from the profit of the house. It-- it was, of course, mortgaged somewhat, by that time. Originally, it was owned clear. And they paid us enough so we can make a living, and take it off at the end, when they would sell it, for what my mother-- my mother's share would have been. So we made a living. A-- a bare living, but we made a living. And I'm not complaining. Still, we were not short of food. We had a roof over our head, and we had clothes to wear, so I mean-- so my father did work, keeping busy. He worked there voluntarily, and it kept him busy. Then they learned-- my parents learned for-- he tried to-- he tried to leave. He wrote to relatives, he wrote to people we knew. No dice. You're over 35 and you don't know a trade, we don't want you. That was the consulate answer. And relatives-- distant relatives, they promised everything, kept nothing. They were mostly relatives I didn't know. My-- they were cousins of my grandmother, for instance, here in the United States. And they promised everything. Didn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you start realizing what was happening with the Nazis, and what did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, immediately, but particularly when-- when-- when I had to leave school. I enjoyed school. I had no problem in that school. As a matter of fact, two older kids I knew from playground, [INAUDIBLE] they said, if ever anybody-- and both were belonged to the Hitler Youth-- they said, if ever anybody gives you any trouble, let us know in that school. They went-- they went to that school. They were two or three years older than I.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said they belonged to the Hitler Youth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, they did. They-- they had to. They had to, just about. You couldn't [INAUDIBLE] except it. I had a good, good friend, and my mother was very friendly with a-- with a kid from-- I knew from elementary school. And she told us already, right away in the beginning, I put my son into a-- there was a-- The boy scouts, I think, were out by that time. I'm not sure, but they were practically-- they-- they-- they didn't want any-- there were boy scouts in Germany before. It was all Hitler Youth, or the younger version-- Jungvolk, they called it. That was up to 14, and over 14, Hitler Youth. And they said, I put my son into-- the-- there was an organization of-- which represented Germans in foreign countries. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they had a youth organization. I don't know how long they allowed them to stay in there, so she joined. But she told us at that time, if I don't put him in there, he has to join the Nazis, and she was no Nazi. But what later happened, I don't know. I lost sight of-- of-- of that kid, and we moved out of the neighborhood, too. So probably had to join the Nazis eventually, anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you're saying, at that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] your relationship was very good with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: With-- with the children around me? Absolutely. No bad-- no bad relationship. I had no-- there were some-- I had an incident. And this-- this fellow was three or four years older than I am. He was some kind of leading figure in the Hitler Youth. And he got into a fight with a kid his age from another-- they had, where we lived, they had these playgrounds. They had a whole bunch of these-- these-- these three-sided apartment houses-- three sides. The fourth side was open at the time. And they had several other courts. Now, we played with ours. The others were the others. We had nothing to do with them. And one kid came over, and made some antisemitic remarks, and this guy started a fight with him. Himself, a Naz-- a Nazi follower, but not an antisemite. Took-- he-- he made some nasty remarks to-- at me, and this guy was around, and he literally threw him out of our-- of our backyard. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, I'm saying the truth. This is-- this was my experience with some people. Things changed after that, though. That was 1933, only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall your friend's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: This particular man's name, I recall. This particular fellow's name, I recall. His name was Heinz Meisner. How do I know-- if he's still alive, I don't know. But his name, I recall. And as far as I know, he-- I think he-- had-- I don't think he had a father, or his father wasn't around. He was a very-- very nice fellow. He was three, four years older. When you're 10, and they're 13 or 14, it makes a lot of difference. You look up to them. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see any book burnings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Book burnings.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not personally, no. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they were burned. I mean-- I mean, I-- I-- I do believe I may have seen it in news reels. I'm not sure. I mean, I'm sure they-- they were-- they-- they prided themselves for burning books. So no, I didn't see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any Jewish businesses that were boycotted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish businesses that were boycotted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I've seen Jewish business being boycotted. And I've seen, in one case-- where we lived, again, there was soap store. Soap and-- and-- and-- and cleaning supply store. It was a chain. Not necessarily were the employees Jewish in that store. Most of the time, they were not. But there was one lady, which-- which we knew, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who tried to force her way in, by the Nazis, physically. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, they didn't let her. Her husband was a house painter, and he lived around that area, too. And he very often talked to my father. [INAUDIBLE] I'd look out the window, he was in the backyard. They did live there. He must have made, at one time, fairly good money. Rents were high there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day he came up in the uniform-- Nazi uniform. So he tells them, don't look at this, he said-- tells my father. It's what's inside this that counts. He said, I had-- I was out of a job. I have two children to bring up. They offered me a job if I joined the party, so what choice did I have? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is the story. That's the man whose wife tried to break the boycott, only maybe less than a year before. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Neighbors of ours, who were very good friends. The man was a textile salesman. Things were not going good before for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, what am I going to do? I have a chance. I'm sell-- he sell-- he sold Nazi uniforms. He was not a Nazi, but they-- this is how people fell in line, and this is how the people shut their mouths and didn't say anything, even those who were opposed to Nazism.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a good time to change tapes.
